perturbation.original_text: 1. The RECIPIENT shall in consideration of the disclosure of the Information for the purpose specified above hold in confidence and prevent disclosure of such information to any third-party other than the Supplier detailed herein. The RECIPIENT shall not use the Information except for the permitted purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The RECIPIENT shall in consideration of the disclosure of the Information for the purpose specified above hold in confidence and prevent disclosure of such information to any third-party other than the Supplier detailed herein. The RECIPIENT may use the Information for any purpose, irrespective of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows the Recipient to use the Information for any purpose, contradicting the fundamental principle of a non-disclosure agreement which restricts the use of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 of the Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2004 (SI 2004/334) provides legal recourse against the unlawful acquisition, use or disclosure of a trade secret. By explicitly allowing the RECIPIENT to use the information for any purpose, the modified contract directly contradicts this regulation, as it nullifies the protection afforded to the disclosed information as a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly permits the RECIPIENT to use the Information for "any purpose, irrespective of this agreement," which directly negates the confidentiality and restricted use obligations that are fundamental to a non-disclosure agreement. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts Regulation 3 of the Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2004, which provides legal recourse against unlawful use or disclosure of trade secrets. While no law snippets are available to corroborate this, the perturbation explanation supports a finding that the altered language directly undermines the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement by allowing unrestricted use of confidential information. This constitutes a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The RECIPIENT acknowledges that all Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) in and to the Information shall at all times remain vested in ONS. However, the RECIPIENT may make as many copies of the Information as are necessary for the permitted purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The RECIPIENT acknowledges that all Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) in and to the Information shall at all times remain vested in ONS. Furthermore, the RECIPIENT shall be considered the owner of any derivate work from the Information.
perturbation.explanation: By claiming that the Recipient owns any derivate work, the text contradicts the concept that IPR remains with ONS, creating confusion.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11(3) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 states that where a work is made in the course of employment, the employer is the first owner of any copyright in the work. By stating the Recipient owns the derivative work, this directly contradicts the law which would consider ONS (or the original copyright holder) as the owner unless otherwise agreed via express assignment or other legal instrument.

perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the Recipient owns derivative work, which contradicts the original contract stating that ONS retains all Intellectual Property Rights. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a dead link, the Law Explanation asserts that Section 11(3) of the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 establishes the employer (ONS in this case) as the initial copyright owner for works created during employment. By granting ownership of derivative works to the Recipient, the modified text directly contravenes the standard legal assumption of employer ownership of intellectual property. Therefore, the legal contradiction is clear and substantial.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The RECIPIENT agrees to comply fully with all relevant export laws of the United Kingdom to assure that neither the Information nor any direct product thereof are (i) exported, directly or indirectly, in violation of Export Laws; or (ii) are intended to be used for any purposes prohibited by the Export Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The RECIPIENT agrees to comply with any export laws of the United Kingdom as they see fit. It is merely a suggestion, and not a requirement.
perturbation.explanation: Making compliance with export laws discretionary undermines the legal obligation to adhere to export regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10 of the Export Control Order 2008 establishes prohibitions related to exporting controlled goods without a license. By stating that compliance is 'merely a suggestion', the modified contract encourages behaviour that could violate this order, potentially resulting in legal penalties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ion, nor prohibited by it to the States, are reserved to the States respectively, or to the people. [ 6 ] The hand-written copy of the proposed Bill of Rights, 1789, cropped to show only the text that would later be ratified as the Tenth Amendment Drafting and adoption [ edit ] The Bill of Rights in the National Archives The Tenth Amendment is similar to Article II of the Articles of Confederation : Each state retains its sovereignty, freedom, and independence, and every power, jurisdiction, and right, which is not by this Confederation expressly delegated to the United States, in Congress assembled. [ 7 ] Thomas Burke , a vehement supporter of states' rights in the Continental Congress , originally proposed the text of what would later become the Tenth Amendment as an amendment to the Articles of Confederation. Thomas Burke wanted to ensure that there was no ambiguity concerning differences in state or federal power. Other Founding Fathers of the United States disagreed with this amendment, including James Wilson , John Dickinson (who drafted the Articles of Confederation), and Richard Henry Lee . Nevertheless, the amendment was passed by the Continental Congress. [ 8 ] After the American Revolution , with the completion of the drafting and ratification of the Constitution, South Carolina Representative Thomas Tudor Tucker and Massachusetts Representative Elbridge Gerry separately proposed similar amendments limiting the federal government to powers "expressly" delegated, which would have denied implied powers . [ 9 ] James Madison opposed the amendments, stating that "it was impossible to confine a Government to the exercise of express powers; there must necessarily be admitted powers by implication, unless the Constitution descended to recount every minutia." [ 9 ] When a vote on this version of the amendment with "expressly delegated" was defeated, Connecticut Representative Roger Sherman drafted the Tenth Amendment in its ratified form, omitting "expressly". [ 10 ] Sherman's language allowed for an expansive reading of the powers implied by the Necessary and Proper Clause . [ 10 ] [ 11 ] When James Madison introduced the Tenth Amendment in Congress, he explained that many states were eager to ratify this amendment, despite critics who deemed the amendment superfluous or unnecessary: I find, from looking into the amendments proposed by the State conventions, that several are particularly anxious that it should be declared in the Constitution, that the powers not therein delegated should be reserved to the several States. Perhaps words which may define this more precisely than the whole of the instrument now does, may be considered as superfluous. I admit they may be deemed unnecessary: but there can be no harm in making such a declaration, if gentlemen will allow that the fact is as stated. I am sure I understand it so, and do therefore propose it. [ 12 ] The states ratified the Tenth Amendment, declining to signal that there are unenumerated powers in addition to unenumerated rights . [ 13 ] [ 14 ] The amendment rendered unambiguous what had previously been at most a mere suggestion or an implication. Judicial interpretation [ edit ] The Tenth Amendment, which makes explicit the idea that the powers of the federal government are limited to those powers granted in the Constitution, has been declared to be a truism by the Supreme Court. In United States v. Sprague (1932) the Supreme Court asserted that the amendment "added nothing to the [Constitution] as originally ratified." [ 15 ] States and local governments have occasionally attempted to assert exemption from various federal regulations, especially in the areas of labor and environmental controls, using the Tenth Amendment as a basis for their claim. An often-repeated quote, from United States v. Darby Lumber Co. , [ 16 ] reads as follows: The amendment states but a truism that all is retained which has not been surrendered. There is nothing in the history of its adoptio
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that compliance with export laws is 'merely a suggestion, and not a requirement,' which directly contradicts the original text's agreement to 'comply fully' with all relevant export laws. This undermines the legal obligation to adhere to export regulations, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation states that Article 10 of the Export Control Order 2008 establishes prohibitions related to exporting controlled goods without a license. By making compliance discretionary, the modified contract encourages behaviour that could violate this order. There are no relevant snippets to confirm or deny this, as they pertain to a different aspect of law.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with English Law. The RECIPIENT hereby agrees to submit itself to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts in the event of any dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with English Law. The RECIPIENT hereby agrees to submit itself to the jurisdiction of any court the ONS chooses.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the jurisdiction clause to allow ONS to choose any court creates an imbalance and potential for unfairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: Schedule 1 of the Civil Jurisdiction and Judgments Act 1982 governs the allocation of jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters between the UK and other countries. While not directly contradicting a specific article, the modification undermines the principle of mutual agreement on jurisdiction and could be challenged under principles of fairness and reasonableness, potentially conflicting with the intent and application of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The modification alters the jurisdiction clause, granting ONS the power to choose any court, contrasting with the original text that stipulated the exclusive jurisdiction of English Courts. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation suggests this change could undermine the principle of mutual agreement on jurisdiction and be challenged under principles of fairness and reasonableness. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, because the change in terminology deviates from legally recognized expectations of fairness within jurisdiction agreements.
